content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
April 9, 1998
Marianne Milligan
Deputy City Attorney
City of La Habra
Law Office of Richard D. Jones
390 North Brea Boulevard, Suite A
Brea, California 92821
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-98-071
Dear Ms. Milligan:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
1 regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
2 Pursuant to Section 83114 and Regulation 18329 (copies
enclosed), this office may respond to requests for advice only from a person whose duties under
the Act are in question or from that person’s authorized agent. Additionally, under this same
authority, we may not respond with formal, written advice when presented with facts that are
hypothetical and speculative in nature.
You do not indicate in your letter the identity ofthe traffic engineering firms for which
you seek advice nor do you indicate that you are seeking advice at their request and with their
authorization. Because the question you ask centers upon a possible conflict of interest for these
engineering firms, it is imperative that their authorization for advice be explicit. The facts
1 You have actually requested a “formal opinion” from the FPPC concerning your question. An opinion
from the Commission is a formal process whereby one seeks a hearing and written opinion from all of the members
of the Commission as to a particular issue. A request for an opinion is sought upon fourteen days written notice,
and the Executive Director of the Commission has the right to deny any request. (See Regulation 18320, copy
enclosed.) This little-used process is contrasted to the more common process of requesting written advice from the
staff of the Commission. Written requests for advice are typically responded to within 21 business days, and the
written formal advice provided by staff confers immunity upon the requestor as to the acts encompassed by the
request (provided all material facts have been disclosed; see Regulation 18329, copy enclosed). We assume that
despite your actual language, your intention was to request written advice under Regulation 18329. Accordingly,
we are responding with a written advice letter and have not referred this matter to the Executive Director for his
disposition.
2 Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. 1-98-071
Page 2
presented in your letter are also general, culminating in a hypothetical question which references
no specific decision to be considered by the city or to be engaged in by the engineering firms.
Consequently, we are unable to provide formal, written advice in response to your letter.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
LisaL.Ditora
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LLD:tls
I:\Advice Ltrs\98071 .wpd

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor sought advice regarding whether traffic engineering firms would face a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, but did not identify the specific firms or provide authorization from them.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide formal written advice because: (1) the requestor did not indicate the identity of the engineering firms or their explicit authorization for advice, which is required when the question centers on those firms' potential conflicts of interest; and (2) the facts presented were general and hypothetical rather than specific to an actual decision or action.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination letter, not a standard advice letter. No QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections exist in the traditional sense. The substance of the denied question is embedded in the body text explaining why advice cannot be provided. The letter explains the distinction between formal opinions and written advice and notes the requestor's failure to meet jurisdictional requirements.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requestor sought advice regarding whether traffic engineering firms would face a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, but did not identify the specific firms or provide authorization from them.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declined to provide formal written advice because: (1) the requestor did not indicate the identity of the engineering firms or their explicit authorization for advice, which is required when the question centers on those firms' potential conflicts of interest; and (2) the facts presented were general and hypothetical rather than specific to an actual decision or action.

We assume that despite your actual language, your intention was to request written advice under Regulation 18329. embedding.summary: The FPPC declined to provide formal written advice to the Deputy City Attorney of La Habra regarding a potential conflict of interest question involving unidentified traffic engineering firms, citing lack of explicit authorization from the affected parties and the hypothetical nature of the inquiry.
